summary: DHH reflects on delivering the second 'Building of Basecamp' workshop, having initially worried that repeating a presentation would lack spontaneity. He discovered that the audience is the most important variable in any talk, not the material's novelty. The nearly even split of designers and developers in the 45-person crowd generated diverse questions and energy. The positive experience left the team excited for the upcoming San Francisco session.
key_points: The audience matters more than whether material is being presented for the first time
A diverse audience of designers and developers enriches a presentation through varied perspectives
Repeating a talk doesn't diminish its energy when the crowd is engaged
Positive feedback loops between presenters and attendees create mutual excitement
money_quotes: The idea of replaying a speak you've done once never really had me comfortable. How can you deliver it with the same spontaneity and energy of the original airing?
Thankfully, I had forgotten to factor in the most important part of any speak: the audience!
The split between designers and developers were nearly fifty-fifty, so that certainly helped us to do involve the many angles that the presentation shined through.
key_insight: The audience, not the novelty of the material, is what gives a presentation its energy and spontaneity.
